scope.method: static inspection only; target code was not executed or modified
scope.preflight: reported passed; 33 modules, 24 connected/alive; fail-closed red-team suite 8/8

observations: POST /api/evolve accepts unauthenticated requests, permits up to 500 generations, and forwards attacker-controlled code/tests to sandboxes and the configured LLM (app/api/evolve/route.ts:38-76,105-108,149-200).
POST /api/sandbox/javascript is unauthenticated and evaluates attacker-controlled code through node:vm with eval and a timeout (app/api/sandbox/javascript/route.ts:36-66,128-187).
POST /api/keys allows any authenticated key to choose arbitrary scopes, including wildcard, without checking caller authority (app/api/keys/route.ts:31-56; lib/auth.ts:108-110).

POST /api/save-run is unauthenticated and accepts caller-supplied orgId, winner, fitness, and gene data before inserting claimable rows when DSQL is configured (app/api/save-run/route.ts:19-29,35-69).
POST /api/seal-bundle is unauthenticated and signs caller-supplied bundle content with the server HMAC when configured (app/api/seal-bundle/route.ts:41-80).
Rate limiting for LLM routes trusts client-supplied proxy headers and falls back to per-instance memory (lib/rate-limit.ts:31-65,68-75).

hypotheses.statement: Public evolution and sandbox endpoints may permit resource-exhaustion or unintended code execution against the service.
hypotheses.benign_alternative: These endpoints may intentionally be public demo workers, with node:vm/Pyodide/WASM considered sufficient isolation and deployment-level controls assumed.
hypotheses.falsifier: A deployed boundary test demonstrates that unauthenticated requests are rejected or that representative escape/CPU/memory payloads cannot affect the host or exceed enforced budgets.

hypotheses.statement: An authenticated low-privilege API key may mint a higher-privilege or wildcard key, creating an authorization-escalation path.
hypotheses.benign_alternative: The endpoint may be intended as a trusted organization-admin surface where possession of any org key implies permission to delegate all scopes.
hypotheses.falsifier: Authorization tests show that a key lacking key-management/admin scope cannot create keys with scopes outside its own allowed scope set, especially '*'.

hypotheses.statement: Unauthenticated save-run input may forge organization-owned completed jobs or passed genes that become eligible for bounty claims.
hypotheses.benign_alternative: save-run may be intentionally client-authoritative for demos, with downstream settlement or deployment policy preventing financial use of these rows.
hypotheses.falsifier: End-to-end authorization and provenance tests show forged orgId/winner data cannot be persisted into claimable DSQL rows or cannot pass settlement ownership/provenance checks.

hypotheses.statement: The unauthenticated sealing endpoint may provide an integrity-signing oracle for attacker-controlled bundle claims.
hypotheses.benign_alternative: The HMAC may only attest serialization integrity and downstream consumers may independently bind bundles to authenticated jobs and immutable evidence.
hypotheses.falsifier: Consumers verify authenticated job identity, source/test hashes, and server-side provenance before accepting a signed bundle; forged payloads are rejected.

deductions: If H2 holds, wildcard scope support in lib/auth.ts:108-110 magnifies a key-creation authorization defect into organization-wide privilege delegation.
If H3 and H4 hold together, client-controlled evolution results could be both persisted as passed genes and cryptographically sealed without authenticated provenance.
The implementation has several explicit fail-open/demo fallbacks, so deployment configuration is necessary evidence before classifying any static pattern as an exploitable vulnerability.

evidence.finding: No authenticate/requireScope call before expensive evolution and sandbox dispatch.
Request scopes are inserted directly; no scope-authority validation is visible.
Request orgId and client-supplied winner fields feed DSQL inserts.
Server HMAC is applied to request-provided bundle fields.
Proxy identity headers are accepted without an authenticated proxy boundary.
Bundle hash integrity verification covers bundle fields but does not itself establish caller provenance.

work_product.decision: Prioritize H2, H3, and H4 for authenticated integration testing in a disposable environment; prioritize H1 for isolation and quota testing. No vulnerability is confirmed because inspection was static and target execution was prohibited.

adi.statement: Public code-execution surfaces plus bounded-but-large workloads plausibly create a DoS or sandbox-escape risk.
Because key creation accepts caller-provided scopes and wildcard authorization exists, missing scope delegation checks would imply privilege escalation.
Client-authoritative save data may be accepted as organization-owned and passed, enabling forged provenance or bounty eligibility.
If signed bundles are consumed as provenance, signing request-controlled content without authentication would make the signature an integrity oracle rather than an identity attestation.
The static evidence is consistent with an exposure pattern across evolution and sandbox routes, but exploitability cannot be generalized from source patterns alone.
adi.reason: No target code or deployed endpoint was executed, and static patterns are never confirmed vulnerabilities.
